company.name: CBRE Group
company.description: CBRE Group is a commercial real estate services and investment firm providing advisory, property management, project management, facilities management, and real estate investment services.

overview.title: Commercial real estate services platform
paragraphs: CBRE operates a global real estate services platform spanning Advisory Services, Building Operations & Experience, Project Management, and Real Estate Investments. Its 2025 annual report reported total revenue of $40.55 billion, with the largest revenue contribution coming from building operations and facilities-related services.

The company combines broker relationships, local market data, property management operations, mortgage servicing, project delivery, and outsourced workplace operations. That mix makes CBRE less like a pure brokerage and more like a recurring real estate operations and data-services network.

moatNarrative: CBRE's moat comes from scale, local broker and occupier relationships, proprietary transaction context, global delivery capacity, and the ability to bundle consulting, leasing, facilities management, project management, valuation, loan servicing, and investment services. Large corporate occupiers and institutional owners often value accountable service coverage across many markets.

The moat is not absolute because the underlying work is service-heavy and partially local. Open data, tenant-direct tools, owner-operated workplace systems, and specialized regional operators can chip away at individual workflows, but replacing the full global service bundle is harder than replacing any single software layer.
decentralizationNarrative: CBRE is moderately decentralizable at the workflow layer. Property data, maps, maintenance records, project tasks, tenant requests, energy telemetry, and leasing leads can all be coordinated through open or federated systems if owners and occupiers are willing to operate the stack themselves or through smaller local providers.

The least decentralizable parts are trust-heavy advisory, regulated transaction execution, global account management, and the liability-bearing coordination of large commercial portfolios. Decentralized systems can pressure the economics of data access and routine operations before they displace high-stakes brokerage or enterprise outsourcing.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 226-250; refreshed with CBRE FY2025 reporting and current public market-cap references.

moat.rationale: CBRE has global scale, enterprise relationships, a broad service bundle, and substantial recurring outsourcing revenue, but many component workflows are service-based and can be competed away locally or with open software.

decentralizability.rationale: Facilities records, project coordination, mapping, energy telemetry, and marketplace discovery can move to open or federated systems, while regulated transactions and global outsourced account management remain harder to decentralize.

profitability.rationale: CBRE reported $1.157 billion of net income attributable to CBRE Group on $40.55 billion of 2025 revenue, plus $1.92 billion of core adjusted net income, indicating meaningful but service-sector-margin profitability.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate trailing valuation using a public market capitalization around $38 billion and FY2025 net income attributable to CBRE Group of $1.157 billion; this is a rough point-in-time calculation rather than a quoted exchange metric.

marketCap.rationale: Public market-data pages around late May and early June 2026 placed CBRE's market capitalization near $38 billion.

products.name: Advisory & Transaction Services
products.category: Commercial real estate advisory and brokerage
products.summary: CBRE's advisory business supports leasing, capital markets, property sales, mortgage origination, loan servicing, valuation, and related commercial real estate decision-making.
products.whyItMatters: This is the relationship and data layer of commercial real estate, where market access, comparable transactions, tenant demand, and local expertise shape who controls pricing power in property decisions.
replacementSketch: A lighter replacement path would not start by recreating a global brokerage. It would combine open property maps, public records, owner-published availability data, transparent comp databases, and local specialist advisors who compete on execution rather than exclusive information.

The near-term opportunity is narrower but credible: reduce dependence on closed listing, valuation, and workflow channels for smaller owners, community developers, and tenants that can coordinate through open data and portable reputation.

alternatives.name: OpenStreetMap
alternatives.summary: OpenStreetMap is a community-built open geospatial data project that can support property discovery, neighborhood analysis, site context, and local real estate tooling without a proprietary map base.

openness.rationale: OpenStreetMap publishes community-maintained geospatial data under an open data license and has public contribution workflows.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The project lets many contributors maintain shared geographic data rather than relying on one commercial mapping vendor.

readiness.rationale: OpenStreetMap is widely used and mature, though commercial real estate workflows still require additional property, ownership, listing, and transaction layers.

costLeverage.rationale: Open map data can reduce baseline data costs for site analysis and discovery, but specialist data and advisory labor remain significant.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated Commercial Property Market Network
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated property-market network would let landlords, tenants, brokers, appraisers, and lenders publish standardized availability, lease terms, transaction evidence, and reputation records across interoperable servers instead of routing discovery through a few dominant service firms or closed listing channels.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If owners and tenants can exchange verified property, availability, and transaction signals through open protocols, brokerage firms lose some information-control advantage while still competing on negotiation, diligence, and execution quality.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated data publishing and portable reputation. Bitcoin or Lightning could support low-friction paid access to premium diligence packets, but the core mechanism is interoperability rather than monetary settlement.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Owners and brokers publish listings and update status through signed feeds; tenants and advisors subscribe through local or sector-specific servers; appraisers and lenders attach evidence packages; dispute history and fulfilled transactions build portable reputation.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Listings would be signed by the owner or authorized representative, important changes would be timestamped, and transaction claims would require counterparty attestation or document hashes. This constrains spoofing but does not remove the need for legal diligence on title, zoning, and lease terms.
failureModes: Large owners and incumbent brokers may refuse to publish high-quality data into open channels.
False availability, stale terms, and selective disclosure could persist without strong local governance and penalties.
Regulated brokerage, appraisal, and financing requirements vary by jurisdiction and limit full automation.
adoptionPath: Start with community development groups, smaller landlords, coworking operators, and tenant reps that benefit from lower discovery costs.
Add standardized property identifiers, signed availability feeds, and optional paid diligence packets.
Expand into regional commercial real estate cooperatives that share data while preserving local advisory relationships.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly shifts discovery and market evidence from a centralized service relationship toward interoperable publishing by many market participants.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Federated data publishing is technically credible, but real estate participants need incentives to reveal timely, accurate information.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The software layer is feasible using existing geospatial and federation patterns, while legal, data-standard, and adoption barriers are substantial.

incumbentPressure.rationale: It could pressure data access and smaller-market advisory economics, but CBRE's enterprise relationships and execution capacity would remain valuable.

products.name: Global Workplace Solutions
products.summary: CBRE's workplace and building operations services coordinate facilities management, property operations, project delivery, and related outsourced services for occupiers and property owners.
products.whyItMatters: Facilities outsourcing is a recurring operational control point for large real estate portfolios, influencing maintenance data, vendor selection, energy use, workplace experience, and capital project execution.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement would be modular rather than monolithic: open CMMS records, open project management, open IoT telemetry, and local service cooperatives that can bid for maintenance and project work against a shared operating record.
Large enterprises may still hire a prime contractor, but open systems can make property data portable and reduce the switching costs that favor integrated outsourcing firms.

alternatives.name: openMAINT
alternatives.summary: openMAINT is an open source property and facility management CMMS for managing assets, maintenance processes, spaces, and building-related records.

openness.rationale: openMAINT is explicitly positioned as an open source property and facility management solution.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosted facility records improve customer control and portability, though the software itself does not create a decentralized service marketplace.

readiness.rationale: It maps directly to CMMS and facility management needs, but enterprise replacement of a global outsourced provider would require integration, process design, and service partners.

costLeverage.rationale: Open CMMS software can reduce license and switching costs for facility records, while labor and vendor execution remain the main cost base.

alternatives.name: OpenRemote
alternatives.summary: OpenRemote is an open source IoT platform for connecting assets, devices, rules, dashboards, and domain-specific applications, including building management use cases.

openness.rationale: OpenRemote describes its platform as 100% open source and publishes its project repository.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It can help building owners control telemetry and automation data outside a closed vendor stack, though deployments are still usually operated by a specific owner or integrator.

readiness.rationale: The platform is production-oriented for IoT and building management, but replacing a full facilities outsourcing program requires operational partners and integrations.

costLeverage.rationale: Open telemetry and automation can reduce lock-in around building systems and energy management, but hardware, installation, and maintenance remain material costs.

alternatives.name: OpenProject
alternatives.summary: OpenProject is an open source project management platform with planning, scheduling, agile boards, work packages, and collaboration features that can support tenant improvements and facilities project delivery.

openness.rationale: OpenProject is licensed as free and open source software and publishes source code publicly.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosted project coordination improves data sovereignty and vendor portability, but it is not itself a decentralized network.

readiness.rationale: OpenProject is mature project management software, although facilities-specific workflows need configuration and integrations.

costLeverage.rationale: It can reduce project management software lock-in and support owner-controlled delivery records, while professional services and construction costs dominate total project spend.

disruptionConcepts.name: Owner-Controlled Open Facilities Stack
disruptionConcepts.summary: Building owners and occupiers could run an open facilities stack that combines CMMS records, project work packages, IoT telemetry, energy dashboards, vendor directories, and maintenance histories under owner control, making service providers interchangeable against a shared operational record.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If the operating record and telemetry layer belong to the owner or tenant consortium instead of the outsourcing incumbent, facilities management becomes more contestable by local providers, cooperatives, and specialist integrators.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through owner-controlled data, open building telemetry, and cooperative vendor coordination. Bitcoin is not central; payment rails are secondary to reducing data lock-in and making local operators auditable.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Owners maintain shared asset registries and maintenance records; vendors receive work orders and submit completion evidence; IoT systems feed equipment status and energy data; project managers coordinate capital work through open project records.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Work completion can be checked against signed technician logs, before-and-after photos, sensor readings, owner approvals, and retained maintenance histories. Energy and equipment telemetry help detect false reporting, but physical inspections and contractual remedies remain necessary.
failureModes: Enterprises may prefer a single accountable global vendor over coordinating many local providers.
Open systems can fail if asset data is incomplete, poorly governed, or not integrated with finance and procurement systems.
Sensor spoofing, low-quality maintenance evidence, and vendor collusion remain possible without audits.
adoptionPath: Deploy open CMMS and project management for a single owner-controlled portfolio or campus.
Add open IoT telemetry for high-value equipment, energy monitoring, and preventive maintenance.
Form regional vendor cooperatives that bid into the shared system with transparent service histories.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept moves control of facilities records and telemetry from an integrated outsourcing provider to owners and interoperable local operators.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open CMMS, IoT, and project systems provide plausible coordination primitives, but multi-vendor governance is operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The required software categories already exist; the hard work is integration, data cleanup, procurement change, and accountability design.

incumbentPressure.rationale: It can reduce lock-in and enable smaller providers, but CBRE's scale and single-accountability promise remain attractive for large multinational clients.

disruptionConcepts.name: Proof-Backed Local Maintenance Marketplace
disruptionConcepts.summary: A local maintenance marketplace could route facilities work to qualified independent technicians and cooperatives, using portable reputation, signed work evidence, escrowed payments, and sensor-backed completion checks to reduce dependence on a single outsourced facilities manager.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Routine facilities work becomes more contestable when buyers can safely source local operators against transparent evidence and portable reputation instead of buying a bundled global outsourcing relationship.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Lightning can support fast escrow, milestone payments, and small recurring service payments, while decentralized identity and reputation make technician histories portable across platforms. The key role is reducing platform lock-in and payment friction for local operators.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Building owners publish work orders from an open CMMS; verified technicians or cooperatives bid; funds are escrowed; completion evidence is submitted; owner approval, peer review, and sensor data release payment and update reputation.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: The system constrains cheating with signed work orders, geotagged evidence, owner approvals, equipment telemetry where available, dispute bonds, and reputation penalties. It cannot fully eliminate collusion or low-quality work without periodic audits and licensing checks.
failureModes: Insurance, licensing, union, and safety requirements can make open marketplace participation difficult.
Emergency repairs and mission-critical facilities may still require contracted service-level agreements.
Reputation systems can be gamed through collusion, fake reviews, or under-reporting of failures.
adoptionPath: Begin with non-critical maintenance categories for small commercial portfolios and community facilities.
Integrate with open CMMS records and require structured evidence for completion.
Add escrow, dispute bonds, portable credentials, and preferred cooperative networks for higher-value work.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A marketplace with portable reputation and owner-held records directly decentralizes vendor sourcing for routine facilities work.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The workflow is plausible for low-risk tasks, but real-world facilities work has safety, licensing, and accountability constraints.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Software and payment primitives are feasible, but insurance, compliance, verification, and enterprise procurement make broad adoption difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: It would pressure routine local maintenance margins more than CBRE's enterprise-scale outsourcing contracts.
